content.body: Electromagnetic induction is one of the most important discoveries in physics. Michael Faraday (1831) discovered that moving a magnet near a wire coil generates electricity. This principle underlies all electrical generators — turbines that power our cities.

Faraday's Law:
An EMF (electromotive force) is induced in a conductor when:
• The conductor moves through a magnetic field
• The magnetic field through the conductor changes

The size of the induced EMF increases when:
• Speed of movement increases
• Field strength increases
• More turns on the coil
• Using a stronger magnet

Lenz's Law:
The induced current creates a magnetic field that OPPOSES the change that caused it.

Simple rule: the induced current always tries to prevent the change in flux.
This is consistent with conservation of energy — you must do work against this opposition.

content.title: AC Generator (Dynamo)
content.body:
A coil rotates in a magnetic field:
• As coil rotates, flux through it changes → EMF is induced
• Direction of induced current reverses every half turn → AC (alternating current)
• Frequency of AC = speed of rotation (Uganda/UK mains: 50 Hz)
• Slip rings and brushes connect rotating coil to external circuit

Output: sinusoidal wave (alternating between positive and negative)

content.title: Transformers
content.body:
A transformer changes voltage using electromagnetic induction:

Transformer equation:
Vs/Vp = Ns/Np

Vp = primary voltage, Vs = secondary voltage
Np = primary turns, Ns = secondary turns

Step-UP transformer: Ns > Np → increases voltage
Step-DOWN transformer: Ns < Np → decreases voltage

Ideal transformer: Vp×Ip = Vs×Is (power is conserved)

Application: National Grid uses step-up transformers (to reduce energy loss in transmission at high voltage, low current), then step-down to household voltage.

questions.question: Electromagnetic induction is used in:
questions.explanation: Generators convert kinetic energy to electrical energy using electromagnetic induction.

questions.question: The induced EMF increases if you:
options: Increase the number of coil turns
questions.answer: Increase the number of coil turns
questions.explanation: More turns in the coil means more conductors cutting field lines → greater induced EMF.

questions.question: Lenz's Law states that the induced current:
options: Adds to the change causing it
Has no relationship to the causing change
Opposes the change that caused it
questions.answer: Opposes the change that caused it
questions.explanation: Lenz's Law: the induced EMF creates a current that opposes the change in magnetic flux that caused it.

questions.question: An AC generator uses slip rings (not a split-ring commutator) to produce:
questions.explanation: Slip rings allow continuous rotation and the output alternates direction — producing AC.

questions.question: Transformer: Vp=240V, Np=1000, Ns=100. Find Vs.
questions.explanation: Vs/Vp = Ns/Np → Vs = 240 × (100/1000) = 24V. Step-DOWN transformer.

questions.question: A step-up transformer:
questions.explanation: Step-up: Ns>Np → voltage increases. Since power is conserved (IV=const), current decreases.

questions.question: Why does the National Grid transmit electricity at high voltage?
options: To reduce power losses in cables (P=I²R, less current = less loss)
questions.answer: To reduce power losses in cables (P=I²R, less current = less loss)
questions.explanation: P_lost = I²R. High voltage means low current (for same power), dramatically reducing energy lost as heat in transmission cables.

questions.question: Uganda's mains frequency is 50 Hz. This means current reverses direction:
questions.explanation: At 50 Hz, current completes 50 full cycles per second — reversing direction 100 times per second (twice per cycle).

questions.question: Transformer: Vp=12V, Ip=5A. Vs=120V (ideal). Find Is.
questions.explanation: Ideal: VpIp = VsIs → 12×5 = 120×Is → Is = 60/120 = 0.5A.

questions.question: Faraday's discovery (1831) showed that:
options: Changing magnetic field induces EMF in a conductor
questions.answer: Changing magnetic field induces EMF in a conductor
questions.explanation: Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction — a changing magnetic flux through a conductor induces an EMF.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Building on S2, we now explore the mathematical treatment of electromagnetic induction including Faraday's and Lenz's laws and AC generation.

Magnetic flux:
Φ = BA cosθ
B = magnetic field strength (T), A = area (m²), θ = angle between field and normal to area

Faraday's Law:
EMF = −N dΦ/dt

For a rotating coil (AC generator):
EMF = NBAω sin(ωt)
Peak EMF = NBAω
ω = angular velocity (rad/s)

content.title: AC Generator
content.body:
A rectangular coil rotates in a uniform magnetic field.
At θ=0° (coil perpendicular to field): max EMF
At θ=90° (coil parallel to field): zero EMF
Output is sinusoidal: E = E₀ sin(ωt)

To get DC: add commutator to AC generator
Slip rings → AC output; Commutator → DC output

RMS values for AC:
V_rms = V₀/√2 = 0.707 V₀
I_rms = I₀/√2 = 0.707 I₀
Power = V_rms × I_rms

Mains electricity: 240V (rms), 50 Hz

questions.question: Magnetic flux Φ = BA cosθ is maximum when θ =
questions.explanation: Maximum flux when field is perpendicular to coil plane (θ=0, cos0=1). Coil fully 'cuts' field lines.

questions.question: Faraday's law: EMF = −N dΦ/dt means...
options: Larger N and faster flux change → greater EMF
questions.answer: Larger N and faster flux change → greater EMF
questions.explanation: Greater N (turns) and faster rate of flux change both increase induced EMF.

questions.question: In an AC generator, maximum EMF occurs when...
options: Coil is parallel to field (flux changes fastest)
questions.answer: Coil is parallel to field (flux changes fastest)
questions.explanation: When coil plane is parallel to field, field lines cut through at maximum rate → peak EMF.

questions.question: Peak EMF for rotating coil =
questions.explanation: Peak EMF = NBAω where N=turns, B=field, A=area, ω=angular velocity.

questions.question: V_rms relates to peak voltage by...
questions.explanation: RMS (root mean square) voltage = peak voltage / √2 ≈ 0.707 × V₀

questions.question: UK mains electricity is...
questions.explanation: UK mains supply is nominally 230-240V rms at 50 Hz frequency.

questions.question: The difference between AC and DC generators is...
options: DC uses commutator instead of slip rings
questions.answer: DC uses commutator instead of slip rings
questions.explanation: Slip rings maintain contact with rotating coil → AC output. Commutator reverses connections each half-turn → DC output.

questions.question: Lenz's law is a consequence of...
questions.explanation: Lenz's law (opposition to flux change) is required by energy conservation — induced current cannot add energy spontaneously.

questions.question: Eddy currents in transformer cores are reduced by...
options: Laminating the core with insulated layers
questions.answer: Laminating the core with insulated layers
questions.explanation: Thin insulated laminations break up paths for eddy currents, reducing energy loss as heat.

questions.question: A coil has 100 turns, B=0.5T, A=0.02m², ω=100 rad/s. Peak EMF =
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
AC Generator:
EMF = NBAω sin(ωt)
E_max = NBAω
ω = 2πf

Transformer:
Vs/Vp = Ns/Np
Vp×Ip = Vs×Is (ideal)
Step-up: Ns > Np → Vs > Vp
Step-down: Ns < Np → Vs < Vp

content.title: Transformer Calculation
content.body:
Primary: 240V, 1000 turns. Secondary: 50 turns.
Vs = 240 × (50/1000) = 12V (step-down)
If Ip = 0.1A: Is = 240×0.1/12 = 2A

Transformers work only with AC because they need a changing magnetic field to induce EMF. National grid uses high voltage (low current) for efficient transmission, then step-down transformers at homes.

questions.question: AC generator produces EMF using
questions.explanation: Generator converts kinetic energy to electrical energy via electromagnetic induction.

questions.question: EMF of generator is maximum when coil is
questions.explanation: EMF = NBAω sin(ωt): maximum when sin=1, i.e., coil parallel to field, cutting field lines most rapidly.

questions.question: Transformer works with
questions.explanation: Changing current needed to create changing flux needed for induction. DC gives constant flux.

questions.question: Step-up transformer has
questions.explanation: Step-up: Ns > Np → Vs > Vp (voltage increases).

questions.question: Primary 100V, 500 turns. Secondary 2000 turns. Vs =
In ideal transformer, if voltage doubles, current
questions.explanation: Power conserved: P=IV. If V doubles, I must halve.

questions.question: National grid uses high voltage to
questions.explanation: P_loss = I²R. High V → low I → much less power lost in cables.

questions.question: Slip rings in AC generator allow
options: Coil to spin freely while maintaining electrical contact
questions.answer: Coil to spin freely while maintaining electrical contact
questions.explanation: Slip rings allow the rotating coil to connect to external circuit without twisting wires.

questions.question: Frequency of AC generator depends on
questions.explanation: f = rotations per second. Faster rotation = higher frequency AC.

questions.question: Transformer core is laminated to
questions.explanation: Laminated (layered) core reduces eddy currents (wasteful induced currents in core).

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
DC Motor:
- Current-carrying conductor in B field experiences force
- F = BIL (force on conductor)
- Commutator reverses current every half-turn to maintain rotation

Electromagnet strength:
- More turns of coil
- Larger current
- Soft iron core

content.title: Force on Wire
content.body:
Wire of length 0.5m carrying 4A in field of 0.3T.
F = BIL = 0.3 × 4 × 0.5 = 0.6N
Direction: Fleming's Left Hand Rule
Fleming's Left Hand Rule: Point left hand so First finger = Field, seCond finger = Current, thuMb = Motion (force). Used for motors.

questions.question: Force on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field: F =
questions.explanation: F = BIL: magnetic field strength × current × length of conductor.

questions.question: Fleming's Left Hand Rule is for
questions.explanation: Left hand rule gives force direction on conductor (motors). Right hand rule for generators.

questions.question: DC motor uses commutator to
options: Reverse current direction every half rotation
questions.answer: Reverse current direction every half rotation
questions.explanation: Commutator ensures coil always rotates same way by reversing current direction.

questions.question: To increase force on motor coil
questions.explanation: F = NBIL - increasing B (stronger magnet) increases force.

questions.question: Electromagnet is stronger with
questions.explanation: Soft iron core concentrates and amplifies magnetic field. Soft iron is easily magnetised and demagnetised.

questions.question: Soft iron vs steel for electromagnet core
questions.explanation: Soft iron magnetises/demagnetises easily (temporary magnet) - needed to turn electromagnet on/off.

questions.question: Electric bell uses
options: Electromagnet that repeatedly makes and breaks circuit
questions.answer: Electromagnet that repeatedly makes and breaks circuit
questions.explanation: Bell: electromagnet attracts hammer (rings bell), breaking circuit, demagnetising, spring returns, cycle repeats.

questions.question: A loudspeaker converts
questions.explanation: Loudspeaker: varying current in coil (in permanent magnet field) causes cone to vibrate, producing sound.

questions.question: Relay is an electromagnetic
questions.explanation: Relay: small current energises electromagnet which attracts armature to close high-current contacts.

questions.question: Magnetic field inside a solenoid is
questions.explanation: Inside solenoid, field lines are parallel to axis and uniform (similar to bar magnet).

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Electromagnetic Induction. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Electromagnetic Induction concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Electromagnetic Induction builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Electromagnetic Induction. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Electromagnetic Induction. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Electromagnetic Induction include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Electromagnetic Induction. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
